Alternatively, perhaps I can collect the factors in two separate lists: one for the lower half and one for the higher half. Then, the lower list is in order, and the higher list is in reverse order. Then, when I merge them, I can get the sorted list.

So the approach is:

1. Initialize two vectors: lower and higher.
2. Loop i from 1 to sqrt(n). For each i:
   a. If n % i == 0:
      i. Add i to lower.
      ii. If i != n/i, add n/i to higher.
3. After the loop, reverse the higher vector.
4. Concatenate lower and higher to get the sorted factors list.

This way, we avoid having to sort all factors, which would take O(m log m) time where m is the number of factors. Instead, this approach is O(sqrt(n)) time, which is better for larger n.

Now, let's write the code.

In the starter code, the function is inside a class. So the code would be:

#include <vector>
#include <algorithm> // for reverse

using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    int kthFactor(int n, int k) {
        vector<int> lower, higher;
        for (int i = 1; i * i <= n; ++i) {
            if (n % i == 0) {
                lower.push_back(i);
                if (i != n / i) {
                    higher.push_back(n / i);
                }
            }
        }
        reverse(higher.begin(), higher.end());
        vector<int> factors;
        factors.insert(factors.end(), lower.begin(), lower.end());
        factors.insert(factors.end(), higher.begin(), higher.end());
        if (factors.size() >= k) {
            return factors[k-1];
        } else {
            return -1;
        }
    }
};

Efficiency-wise, this is O(sqrt(n)) time, which is better than O(n). So for the follow-up, yes, it's solved in less than O(n) time.

So the solution is efficient and correct.

solution:
```cpp
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>

using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    int kthFactor(int n, int k) {
        vector<int> lower, higher;
        for (int i = 1; i * i <= n; ++i) {
            if (n % i == 0) {
                lower.push_back(i);
                if (i != n / i) {
                    higher.push_back(n / i);
                }
            }
        }
        reverse(higher.begin(), higher.end());
        vector<int> factors;
        factors.insert(factors.end(), lower.begin(), lower.end());
        factors.insert(factors.end(), higher.begin(), higher.end());
        if (factors.size() >= k) {
            return factors[k-1];
        } else {
            return -1;
        }
    }
};
```

efficiency: HIGH

time complexity: O(sqrt(n))

space complexity: O(sqrt(n))

energy implications: LOW

explanation: The solution efficiently finds factors by iterating up to sqrt(n), collecting factors in two lists (lower and higher), then combining and reversing the higher list to form the sorted factors. This approach ensures we avoid the O(n) complexity and sorting step, making it optimal for the problem constraints.
